coredata.dc:title: The inhibition performance of novel amino acid-based amphiprotic surfactants on aluminum alloys in sodium chloride solutions: Experimental and theoretical studies
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jinyan
Zheng, Tianyu
Wang, Jia
Jia, Guixiao
coredata.dc:description: The inhibition performance of three amino acid-based amphoteric surfactants disodium-N-alkyliminodiacetate was investigated by the weight-loss and electrochemical methods. XPS and AFM were also utilized to study the alloy of microscopic variation. Meanwhile, the anti-corrosion performance and mechanism were verified by quantum chemical calculation and molecular dynamics simulation. It found that the inhibition efficiency increased with the surfactant concentration, which peaked above 90% at the critical micelle concentration and decreased at higher concentrations due to the micelle formation. The inhibition efficiency of surfactants with longer carbon chains increased due to the thicker and more hydrophobic adsorption layer formed on alloy surface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiprotic surfactant
Corrosion inhibitor
Aluminum alloy
Sodium chloride medium
Theoretical calculation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurements of critical micelle concentrations of the surfactants

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 21463016 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Nyquist plot, (b, c) Bode plots and phase plots for AA2024-T3 in 3.5% sodium chloride solution without and with various concentrations of RNC-14, (d) The equivalent circuit.
Nyquist plots for AA2024-T3 with different concentrations of surfactants in 3.5% sodium chloride solution: (a) RNC-8, (b) RNC-12.
Polarization curves for AA2024-T3 without and with surfactants at different concentrations in 3.5% sodium chloride solution: (a) RNC-8, (b) RNC-12, and (c) RNC-14.
XPS spectra for the aluminum alloy with RNC-14 at concentration 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 in 3.5% sodium chloride solution immersed for 24h
AFM images of AA2024-T3 (a) without inhibitor, (b) with RNC-8, (c) with RNC-12, (d) with RNC-14.
Adsorption isotherm plots of (a) RNC-8, (b) RNC-12, (c) RNC-14.
The optimized geometric structures and frontier molecule orbital density distributions of three inhibitors.
The frontier orbitals energetic positions diagram of three inhibitors.
The adsorption models of RNC-n (with n = 8, 12, 14) inhibitors on Al(1 1 1): (a)Vacuum, (b)Aqueous solvent.
The cmc of different surfactants in the sodium chloride solution.
The IE 1% of the surfactants at different concentrations by loss weight methods.
The electrochemical parameters of AA2024-T3 with different concentrations of surfactants in 3.5% sodium chloride solution.
Polarization parameters for AA2024-T3 with surfactants at different concentrations in 3.5% sodium chloride solution.
XPS analysis results of pure RNC-14 and the aluminum alloy with RNC-14.
Thermodynamic parameters for the aluminum alloy in 3.5% NaCl in the presence of inhibitors at 303K
Adsorption energies of different corrosion inhibitors (RNC-8, 12, 14) on Al(111) Surface.

head.ce:title: The inhibition performance of novel amino acid-based amphiprotic surfactants on aluminum alloys in sodium chloride solutions: Experimental and theoretical studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, Baotou, 014010, Inner Mongolia, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering , Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, Baotou 014010, Inner Mongolia, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Metallurgy, Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, Baotou, 014010, Inner Mongolia, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Metallurgy, Inner Mongolia University of Science & Technology, Baotou 014010, Inner Mongolia, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inhibition performance of three amino acid-based amphoteric surfactants disodium-N-alkyliminodiacetate was investigated by the weight-loss and electrochemical methods. XPS and AFM were also utilized to study the alloy of microscopic variation. Meanwhile, the anti-corrosion performance and mechanism were verified by quantum chemical calculation and molecular dynamics simulation. It found that the inhibition efficiency increased with the surfactant concentration, which peaked above 90% at the critical micelle concentration and decreased at higher concentrations due to the micelle formation. The inhibition efficiency of surfactants with longer carbon chains increased due to the thicker and more hydrophobic adsorption layer formed on alloy surface.

ce:section.ce:para: Aluminum and its alloys have attracted much attention due to its remarkable qualities, such as low costs, light weights and excellent electrical conductivities. They have found applications in many areas including motor vehicles, ships, machineries, manufacture and heavy industrial equipment [1–3]. In the manufacturing process of heavy industries, heat transfer has frequently occurred in cooling systems, which raises up the temperature of equipment and indirectly reduces the service life and production efficiency. Fresh water has been mainly used to assimilate the redundant heat [4]. However, this leads to the large consumption of fresh water. Given the already limited supply of fresh water owing to the exploding world population, alternative approaches are highly demanded. One alternative and effective approach to reduce heat in cooling water systems is to utilize seawater [5]. However, the major drawback of the approach is the serious corrosion occurred on the alloy surface due to the presence of Cl− in the seawater [6]. Thus, it is vital to seek suitable methods to increase the corrosion resistance of aluminum and its alloys while using seawater.

It is well known that adding corrosion inhibitors is effective against seawater corrosions. Additionally, it has offered many advantages, such as low costs, simple operation and low environmental impacts [7–11]. Among them, organic inhibitors have become more and more popular as effective metal corrosion inhibitors because of the presence of heteroatoms such as nitrogen [12], sulfur [13], phosphorus [14], or oxygen [15], unsaturated bonds [16] and aromatic rings [17,18]. In general, the inhibition efficiency of a corrosion inhibitor is closely related to its adsorption capacity which in turn is determined by its chemical structure, surface charge and electronic density. Among organic corrosion inhibitors, organic surfactants are one important class of inhibitors that can interact with metals to form protective films to block active sites and decrease corrosion rates [19–22].

Recently, some surfactants have been used as corrosion inhibitors for the 2024 aluminum alloy in sodium chloride media. Balbo et al. [23] investigated the inhibition efficiency of sodium salts of N-lauroylsarcosine (NLS), N-lauroyl-N-methyltaurine (NLT), dodecylbenzenesulfonic acid (DBS), and sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS). Boisier and Wan Nik [24] studied the corrosion inhibition of 2024 aluminum alloy by sodium decanoate and sodium benzoate. It is well known that monocarboxylic acidic surfactants (CH3-(CH2)n-COOH) can act as corrosion inhibitors for aluminum alloys [25]. Landry et al. have used long-chain organic acids to create a hydrophobic film on various aluminum alloy surfaces [26,27]. These substances demonstrated great corrosion inhibition effects on aluminum alloys. Recent publications reported that six kinds of hydroxyl carboxylic acids have also shown corrosion inhibition effects on the aluminum surface [28–31].

Moreover, N-Acyl amino acid surfactants attract tremendous interest in the field of surfactant inhibitors because they have excellent surface-active properties, fast biodegradation and low toxicities compared to conventional surfactants [32]. It has been found out that the thickness of hydrophobic film formed by corrosion inhibitors is critical for the inhibition efficiency and depends on the carbon chain length [33,34]. However, the impact of chain length of surfactants on the corrosion inhibition has seldomly been reported in the literature.

Therefore, in this work, amino acid-based amphoteric surfactants disodium-N-alkyliminodiacetate (CnH2n+1N(CH2COONa)2, RNC-n, with n = 8, 12, 14) as corrosion inhibitors for aluminum alloy 2024-T3 (AA2024-T3) in a sodium chloride medium were systematically investigated by static weight loss methods, electrochemical measurements, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and atomic force microscope (AFM). Ultimately, quantum chemical calculation and molecular dynamics simulation were utilized to illustrate the inhibition mechanism.
The composition of the AA2024-T3 is as follows: 0.50% Fe, 0.50% Si, 4.18% Cu, 0.30% Mn, 0.30% Zn, and 1.30–1.80% Mg, with the remainder being Al.

Three inhibitors RNC-n (n = 8, 12, 14) were purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd., and the chemical structures were shown in Fig. 1 . The rest of chemicals (ethanol, acetone, ethylenediamine, epoxy resin andsodium chloride) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

The surface tension was measured by a surface tensiometer (BZY-2, Shanghai Hengping Instrument and Meter Factory, China) using the Wilhelmy plate method. This instrument has a resolution of 0.01 mN/m, an accuracy of 0.02 mN/m, and a measurement range of 0–200 mN/m. A Wilhelmy plate (24 mm × 10 mm × 0.2 mm) made of platinum was selected in experiment.

The AA2024-T3 slices of 20 mm × 30 mm × 3 mm were abraded with a series of emery papers (grades 120, 240, 400, 600, 800 and 1200), then washed with distilled water and acetone. After weighing, the samples were immersed for 24 h in solutions contained 3.5% sodium chloride either without inhibitors or with inhibitors at concentrations from 1 × 10−5 mol L−1 to 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 [35,36]. All tests were maintained at 30 °C. The corrosion rate (W) was calculated from the weight loss measurements according to equation (1):
(1)
W =
m 1
−
m 2
S t
Where m 1 and m 2 were the masses of alloy before and after soaking, respectively, S was the surface area of AA2024-T3, and t was the immersing time. The inhibition efficiency (IE 1%) was determined using equation (2):
(2)
I
E 1
% =
W 0
−
W i
W 0
× 100 %
Where W 0 and W
i were corrosion rates without and with corrosion inhibitors, respectively.

Electrochemical tests were carried out in a convenient workstation (AutoLab302 N) with a typical three-electrode arrangement, including saturated calomel (the reference electrode), platinum sheet (a counter electrode) and AA2024-T3 sheet (the working electrode). The working electrode had an uncovered surface of 1 cm2 with the remaining surface covered with epoxy resin and ethylenediamine. In order to make the working electrodes reach a relatively stable state, they were immersed into NaCl solutions for 24 h before tests. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured at frequencies from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz with potential amplitude of 10 mV. The potentiodynamic polarization curves were measured in the potential range from +350 mV to −150 mV at a sweep rate of 1.0 mV s−1.

The morphologies of the corroded surfaces in the presence and absence of surfactants were studied after immersed in NaCl solutions for 24 h. The analysis of surface morphology was carried out using AFM (Dimension Icon Bruker). The elemental compositions of inhibitor films on metal surfaces were analyzed with XPS analysis (Escalab 250Xi).

In general, a corrosion inhibitor could protect metal from corrosions only when it is adsorbed on the desired metal surface. Basically, the adsorption capacity of a molecule is closely related to its structure. Therefore, the density functional theory (DFT) was employed via Dmol3 module with GGA/PW91 (DNP basis set) for all atoms in materials studio 6.0 to study the structure of corrosion inhibitors [37]. The quantum-chemical properties, such as energies of the highest occupied molecular orbitals (E HOMO), energies of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (E LUMO), and dipole moments (μ) were calculated.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation was carried out using Forcite module to predict interactions between inhibitors and the metal surface [38]. The Al(111) surface was selected for its high stabilization energy to reflect the realistic condition better. The calculation was done in vacuum or with 300 water molecules, along with one anionic inhibitor molecule (RCN-n, n = 8, 12, 14) [39,40]. The simulation was done using the COMPASS force field and the NVT canonical ensemble at 303 K temperature. In calculation, all aluminum atoms were immobilized and other molecules were in a flabby status. The potential energies of the whole system were minimized for at least 5000 steps by means of Smart algorithm before simulation. Electrostatic, non-bonding, and van der Waals interactions were set as atom-based summations using the Ewald summation method and a cut-off radius of 18.50 Å. The simulation time was 500 ps with a time step of 1.0 fs. Interaction energy estimation was calculated by using the following equation [41]:
(3)
E Intraction
=
E Total
−
(
E
Surface + Water
+
E
Water + Inhibitor
)
+
E Water
where
E Total
was the energy of the whole system,
E
Surface + Water
was the energy of metal surface and water, was the energy of water and RNC-12 and
E Water
was the energy of water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of critical micelle concentrations of the surfactants
ce:section.ce:para: The critical micelle concentrations (cmc) of various surfactants in the sodium chloride solution were estimated by the surface tension method and the results were shown in Table 1 . It was shown that the value of the cmc decreased with the increase of hydrophobic chain lengths, which was attributed to the increase of alkyl carbon chain length led to the decrease of solubility.

Table 2 showed the values of IE 1% procured by the weight loss measurements of the surfactants at various concentrations in the sodium chloride solution. It was clearly shown that IE 1% increased when surfactant concentrations increasing from 1 × 10−5 mol L−1 to 1 × 10−4 mol L−1. Taking RNC-14 as an example, the maximum IE 1 was 77.71% at the concentration of 1 × 10−4 mol L−1, which was the cmc of the inhibitor. Then the IE 1% decreased with surfactant concentrations increasing above 1 × 10−4 mol L−1. Additionally, at the concentration of 1 × 10−4 mol L−1, the IE 1% decreased in the following order: RNC-14 > RNC-12 > RNC-8. It indicated that the excellent corrosion inhibition performance was closely related to the length of carbon chain. The longer the carbon chain was, the better the corrosion inhibition performance was.

Fig. 2 displayed a typical series of Nyquist plot, Bode and Phase angle plots for AA2024-T3 in the absence or the presence of RNC-14 at various concentrations. The diameter capacitive loop usually is related to the charge transfer of the corrosion process [42]. The diameter of the capacitive loop of RNC-14 increased with the increasing concentrations (Fig. 2 a). The diameter capacitive loop might result from the adsorption of the inhibitor on the metal surface [43,44]. The impedance modulus increased at first and then decreased with the increase of concentration at the lower frequency region (0.1 Hz < f < 1 Hz), which was consistent with the diameter of the capacitive loop. The Nyquist plot was corresponding to a one time constant, which was consistent with the Bode and phase angle plots (Fig. 2 b). Moreover, the phase angle values (Fig. 2 b) became larger with the increase of concentration, which was attributed to the adsorption of inhibitors on the alloy surface. An equivalent electrical circuit was also established (Fig. 2 c). R s and R p were the solution resistance and the charge transfer resistance, respectively. The Constant Phase Angle Element (CPE) was the double layer capacitance. n (0 ≤ n ≤ 1) was the coefficient related to deviation (When n = 1 or n = 0, CPE is “pure capacitance” or “pure resistance”).

The fitting values were listed in Table 3 , indicating that the variation trends of the surfactants were the same. For example, when the surfactant concentration of RNC-14 was below 1 × 10−4 mol L−1, the value of CPE decreased with the concentration of RNC-14 increasing, which could be attributed to the surface coverage. The CPE values decreased as the concentration changed from 7.46 × 10−5 μF cm−2 to 3.79 × 10−5 μF cm−2. That was because water molecules were replaced by inhibitor molecules. The increase of R p values from 2113 Ω cm2 to 4752 Ω cm2 was attributed to the protective film on the metal surface. R p values were used to calculate the IE 2% according to the following expression [45]:
(4)
I
E 2
% =
( 1 −
R p 0
R p
)
× 100 %
Where
R p 0
and
R p
were the charge transfer resistance values without and with inhibitors, respectively.

Table 3 implied that the IE 2% increased with concentrations up to 1 × 10−4 mol L−1, and the maximum efficiency reached 90% at 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 for all three inhibitors. However, when the concentration reached the cmc value, micelles would form in the solution. The number of free surfactant molecules in the sodium chloride solution decreased, and the molecules that could adsorb on the metal surface accordingly decreased. Table 3 showed that the values of R p decreased and the values of CPE increased, when surfactant concentrations increased above 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 and exceeded the cmc of the surfactants. It indicated that the reduction of surface coverage led to the decrease of IE 2%. In addition, it seemed obvious that the IE 2% increased when inhibitors had longer chain lengths in Fig. 3 and Table 3. At the concentration of 1 × 10−4 mol L−1, the values of the IE 2% were 90.02%, 90.89% and 92.19% for RNC-8, RNC-12 and RNC-14, respectively.

The Tafel plots of the potentiodynamic polarization without and with surfactants at various concentrations were performed and shown in Fig. 4 . The parameters obtained from the Tafel plots included the corrosion current density (I corr), the corrosion potential (E corr), the anodic slopes (β a) and cathodic slopes (β c) [46,47]. The IE 3% values were calculated from equation (5) [48]:
(5)
I
E 3
% =
( 1 −
I corr 0
I corr
)
× 100 %
Where
I corr 0
and
I corr
were the corrosion current densities in the presence or absence of inhibitors, respectively. The corrosion parameters from the potentiodynamic polarization studies were summarized in Table 4 .

In general, the protective films (aluminum hydroxide) will form on the surface of aluminum alloy due to the presence of water molecules and oxygen in corrosion solutions. The reaction equations (6) and (7) are as follows:
(6)
A n o d e : 4 Al = 4
Al
3 +
+ 12
e −
(7)
C a t h o d e : 3
O 2
+ 6
H 2
O + 12
e −
= 12
OH −

With the further increase of immersion time, the chloride ion with strong activation in sodium chloride will gradually replace the hydroxide ion in aluminum hydroxide to form a new corrosion product (aluminum chloride). The reaction (8), (9), (10) and (11) are as follows:
(8)
Al
( OH )
3
+
Cl −
=
Al
( OH )
2
Cl +
OH −
(9)
Al
( OH )
2
Cl +
Cl −
= Al
( OH )
Cl 2
+
OH −
(10)
Al
( OH )
Cl 2
+
Cl −
=
AlCl 3
+
OH −
(11)
AlCl 3
+
Cl −
=
[
AlCl 4
]
−

The aluminum ions chlorinated by chloride ion continue to dissolve, resulting in further corrosion of the aluminum alloy matrix. Based on Fig. 4 and Table 4, compared to the E corr without corrosion inhibitor (−681.92 mV), E corr of all inhibitors drifted in a negative direction, which was attributed to the heterogeneous adsorption of the corrosion inhibitor on the electrode surface, resulting in different degrees of electrochemical inhibition of cathodic hydrogen evolution and anodic dissolution [47]. For instance, the value of E corr was −722.73 mV with RNC-14 at the concentration of 1 × 10−4 mol L−1. The difference of E corr values was 40.81 mV, which was less than 85 mV [49]. It indicated RNC-14 acted as a mixed type of inhibitor. Similarly, RNC-8 and RNC-12 were also proved to be mixed inhibitors.

Besides, after adding corrosion inhibitor, the change of β a value (from 8.98 mV·dec−1 to 16.78 mV·dec−1) was more conspicuous than that of β c value without inhibitors (from 141.28 mV·dec−1 to 362.62 mV·dec−1). Thus, cathodic suppression was dominant in the processes. Compared with Blank, the shape of the polarization curve of the inhibitor does not change. The Tafel curves showed that the changes of β c value might be attributed to the adsorption of the inhibitors onto the metal surface. This fact proved that the surfactant primarily blocked the active sites rather than changing the anodic and cathodic electrochemical reactions.

In addition, the resistance of the corrosion electrochemical reaction on the electrode surface increased owing to the corrosion inhibitors covering the metal surface. Taking RNC-14 as an example, the I corr values decreased from 18.79 μA cm−2 to 8.30 μA cm−2 with surfactant at various concentrations. The IE 3% increased from 82.46% to 92.25%. However, I corr increased further to 9.16 μA cm−2 while IE 3% decreased down to 91.45% as the concentration further went up to 5 × 10−4 mol L−1. As discussed above, the formation of micelles reduced the number of free surfactant molecules and thus resulted in the decrease of IE 3%.

Comparing the three inhibitors at the same concentration, taking 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 as an example, I corr decreased and IE 3% increased with the increase of the hydrophobic chain length. It indicated that the longer chain length resulted in the thicker adsorption layer on the metal surface, and thus the IE 3% in sodium chloride solutions was higher. To sum up, due to the heterogeneous adsorption of corrosion inhibitors on the metal surface, the electrochemical reaction between cathode and anode is inhibited, thus slowing down the corrosion of the alloy in sodium chloride solution.

Fig. 5 were XPS spectra of the components of pure RNC-14 and aluminum alloy with RNC-14 at a concentration of 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 in sodium chloride solutions. Compared with pure RNC-14, the presence of elements, such as C, O, N, proved that RNC-14 absorbed on the alloy surface after immersion. The peaks, chemical bonds and corresponding elements were listed in Table 5 .

The elements of C were represented by three peaks. The peaks for pure RNC-14 at 284.30 eV, 286.20 eV, 289.00 eV could be attributed to C–N, C–H/C–C and O–CO, respectively. Similarly, the peaks for alloy surface at 285.50 eV, 286.20 eV and 289.00 eV also corresponded to the above three chemical bonds. The O element also contained three peaks. For pure RNC-14, the first peak at 530.25 eV was O–H, the second peak at 531.28 eV was COONa and the last peak at 534.84 eV was O–C. After treatment of the metal alloy with the inhibitor, the peaks at 523.60 eV, 533.30 eV and 537.90 eV were O–Al, OC–O, O–H. The peaks of N element for pure RNC-14 at 398.24 eV and 400.1 eV was C–N and NR3 in alkyl groups, while the peak on alloy surface at 400.7 eV was C–N. Besides, the peaks of Na elements for pure RNC-14 and the alloy surface at 1070.18 eV and 1073.80 eV corresponded to the Na–O bond. In consequence, the existence of these elements and chemical bonds proved that the inhibitors were adsorbed on the metal surface.

The surface morphology was analyzed using AFM technology 2D, 3D and section images were shown in Fig. 6 (a–d). The average surface roughness of AA2024-T3 immersed in 3.5% NaCl solution without inhibitors was 58.3 nm, which was larger than ones with inhibitors. The heights of peaks and valleys were approximately 234.5 nm and −168.6 nm. This data indicated that a series of severe corrosion reactions had happened on the alloy surface. On the contrary, after adding various inhibitors (RNC-8, RNC-12, RNC-14) at optimal concentrations, the average surface roughness dropped to 21.0 nm, 17.7 nm and 16.2 nm, respectively. The peaks and valleys tended to be horizontal. It considerably attenuated the fluctuation degree of the alloy surface and alleviated corrosions. This indicated that all three kinds of inhibitors, especially RNC-14, could seclude the metal from solutions and achieve anti-corrosion effects. The AFM results further confirmed the order of inhibition efficiency obtained from the weight loss and electrochemical measurements.

The isothermal adsorption plots of the three corrosion inhibitors were shown in Fig. 7 . The corrosion inhibition adsorption process in the current study can be represented by equation (12) [65]:
(12)
C Inhibitor
θ
=
C Inhibitor
+
1
K Ads
Where C Inhibitor was the inhibitor concentration, θ was the fractional coverage value (θ = IE%/100, IE% was average value obtained from three methods including weight loss, EIS and potentiodynamic polarization curve), and K Ads was the equilibrium constant for the adsorption process.

The linear fitting and regression coefficients of three corrosion inhibitors (R2 = 0.99966, R2 = 0.99972, R2 = 0.99998) suggested that the experimental data conformed to the Langmuir isotherm in the sodium chloride solution. With the intercept of the C/θ-C diagram, the Gibbs free energy of adsorption (ΔG Ads) value could be calculated by equation (13) [66]:
(13)
Δ
G Ads
= − R T
ln
( 55.5
K Ads
)
where T was the temperature (303.15 K), and the constant value (55.5) was the molar concentration of water.

The values of K Ads and ΔG Ads were listed in Table 6 . It had been observed that ΔG Ads values were negative for the adsorption processes of all three inhibitors on the alloy surface. Moreover, negative ΔG Ads values indicated that the adsorption process was spontaneous. Meanwhile, ΔG Ads values of the inhibitors were −42.46 kJ mol−1, −42.51 kJ mol−1, −50.06 kJ mol−1 respectively, whose absolute values were more than 40 kJ mol−1, indicating that all three corrosion inhibitors had adsorbed onto the alloy surface chemically in the process of the metal corrosion inhibition [67].

The frontier molecule orbital density distributions of three inhibitors with various carbon chain lengths were calculated, and the results were presented in Fig. 8 . It was heterogeneous distribution for the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) that existed in O atoms, C–O and C–C in the sodium acetate, and C atoms at the end of hydrophobic chain, respectively.

Table 7 showed the quantum chemical calculation parameters of the different inhibitors. Frontier orbital energies could be used to identify reactivity centers of molecules [68]. It has been reported that efficient corrosion inhibitors did not only provide electrons to unoccupied orbitals of the metal but also accept free electrons from the metal [69]. According to the frontier orbit theory [70,71], the reaction activity of molecules is closely related to E HOMO and E LUMO, which reflect the ability of molecules to contribute/receive electrons, respectively. The higher E HOMO values, the higher electron supply capacities which could bond with the empty p orbitals on the aluminum surface. Molecules with lower E LUMO values had better electron acceptance capacities which made it easier for inhibitors to accept electrons from the aluminum surface and form an anti-bonding. In addition, the energy gap (ΔE = E LUMO-E HOMO) could be used to evaluate the intensity of the bond between the molecule and metal [72]. The smaller the ΔE value was, the less stable the inhibitor molecule was and the easier the reaction with the metal was. In Figs. 8 and 9 , with the increase of carbon chain lengths, the energy gap difference decreased, indicating that the reaction between RNC-14 and aluminum surface was the strongest. Dipole moment (μ) is a polarity parameter that is related to electrons distribution in molecules [73]. In general, the higher μ values would make it easier for inhibitors to adsorb on the metal surface [74]. It can be seen from the table that the dipole moments were in the following order: RNC-8 < RNC-12 < RNC-14. Therefore, the inhibition efficiency of RNC-14 was the highest.

Furthermore, the molecular dynamics simulation was performed to investigate the interaction between the inhibitors and AA2024-T3 surface. The equilibrium configurations (top and side view) of inhibitors on Al(111) surface were exhibited in Fig. 10 .

It could be observed in Fig. 10 that each inhibitor was adsorbed on the surface of Al(111) with a parallel mode, which could protect the AA2024-T3 in the corrosion media effectively. The relevant parameters of the RNC-n (n = 8, 12, 14) molecules were listed in Table 8 . The more negative value of interaction energies (E Interaction), the easier the adsorption on the surface of AA2024-T3 was [75,76]. The order of three inhibitors (RNC-8 < RNC-12 < RNC-14) indicated that the increase of carbon chain lengths was beneficial to the corrosion inhibition performance [77]. This was consistent with the previous corrosion inhibition efficiency experiments.

In general, due to the existence of Cl ions, the anodic surface of aluminum alloy will continue to dissolve. Hydrogen will also precipitate from the cathode surface of the alloy due to the presence of H2O molecules. These chemical reactions destroy the original structure of the alloy surface, resulting in a significant decline in its properties. However, the molecular structures of RNC-n (n = 8, 12, 14) homologues contained oxygen and nitrogen with lone pairs of electrons, as well as long carbon chains, which provided the conditions for its adsorption reaction on the alloy surface. The inhibitor molecules could provide these lone-pair electron to the unoccupied p-orbitals of the metal, using the anti-bonding orbitals to form coordinate bonds [78]. In addition, the number of adsorbed molecules increased resulting in the enhancement of the surface coverage. In other words, if inhibitor concentrations increased, more inhibitors would be adsorbed on the surface. These molecules formed a dense protective film on the metal surface, which can prevent the alloy from being corroded by the corrosion medium to a great extent.

However, a decrease of inhibition efficiency was observed when concentrations were slightly above the cmc. This might be attributed to the micelle formation resulting from hydrophobic interactions between the carbon chains (Fig. 11 ), which led to the reduced concentration of free molecules in solution and adsorption molecules on the metal surface accordingly [79]. In addition, there was also competition among the surfactants that have been adsorbed on the metal surface with the continuous increase of inhibitor concentration. The interaction force caused by this competition also led to the partial shedding of the molecules adsorbed on the metal surface resulting in the decline of corrosion inhibition efficiency. Thus, the highest inhibition efficiency could be achieved around cmc concentrations.

In this study, a series of surfactants (RNC-n, n = 8, 12, 14) were investigated both experimentally and theoretically as excellent corrosion inhibitors for AA2024-T3 in sodium chloride solutions. The IE% of surfactants increased with concentrations up to a point but decreased as concentrations increased further over 1 × 10−4 mol L−1. All optimal inhibition efficiencies were approximately 90%. Besides, the corrosion inhibition performance was closely related to the length of the carbon chains. The longer the carbon chain was, the higher the hydrophobicity was, and the better the corrosion inhibition performance was. The enhancement of corrosion inhibition performance was attributed to the inhibitor coverage on the alloy surface rather than the electrochemical property change of the alloy in sodium chloride solutions. All three corrosion inhibitors acted as a mixed inhibitor and primarily suppressed cathodic corrosion reactions. The theoretical simulations were consistent with the experimental results.

Jinyan Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Tianyu Zheng: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, preparation, Review. Jia Wang: Investigation. Guixiao Jia: Formal analysis, Review.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 21463016].
